title: Ensure that VPC Flow Logs is Enabled for Every Subnet in a  VPC Network

input_row.title: Ensure that VPC Flow Logs is Enabled for Every Subnet in a  VPC Network
input_row.description: Flow Logs is a feature that enables users to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in the organization's VPC Subnets. Once a flow log is created, the user can view and retrieve its data in Stackdriver Logging. It is recommended that Flow Logs be enabled for every business-critical VPC subnet.
input_row.rationale: VPC networks and subnetworks not reserved for internal HTTP(S) load balancing provide logically isolated and secure network partitions where GCP resources can be launched. When Flow Logs are enabled for a subnet, VMs within that subnet start reporting on all Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) and User Datagram Protocol (UDP) flows. Each VM samples the TCP and UDP flows it sees, inbound and outbound, whether the flow is to or from another VM, a host in the on-premises datacenter, a Google service, or a host on the Internet. If two GCP VMs are communicating, and both are in subnets that have VPC Flow Logs enabled, both VMs report the flows. Flow Logs supports the following use cases: • Network monitoring • Understanding network usage and optimizing network traffic expenses • Network forensics • Real-time security analysis Flow Logs provide visibility into network traffic for each VM inside the subnet and can be used to detect anomalous traffic or provide insight during security workflows. The Flow Logs must be configured such that all network traffic is logged, the interval of logging is granular to provide detailed information on the connections, no logs are filtered, and metadata to facilitate investigations are included. Note: Subnets reserved for use by internal HTTP(S) load balancers do not support VPC flow logs.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VPC network GCP Console visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/networking/networks/list 2. From the list of network subnets, make sure for each subnet: • Flow Logs is set to On • Aggregation Interval is set to 5 sec • Include metadata checkbox is checked • Sample rate is set to 100% Note: It is not possible to determine if a Log filter has been defined from the console. From Google Cloud CLI gcloud compute networks subnets list --format json | \ jq -r '(["Subnet","Purpose","Flow_Logs","Aggregation_Interval","Flow_Sampling","Met adata","Logs_Filtered"] | (., map(length*"-"))), (.[] | [ .name, .purpose, (if has("enableFlowLogs") and .enableFlowLogs == true then "Enabled" else "Disabled" end), (if has("logConfig") then .logConfig.aggregationInterval else "N/A" end), (if has("logConfig") then .logConfig.flowSampling else "N/A" end), (if has("logConfig") then .logConfig.metadata else "N/A" end), (if has("logConfig") then (.logConfig | has("filterExpr")) else "N/A" end) ] ) | @tsv' | \ column -t The output of the above command will list: • each subnet • the subnet's purpose • a Enabled or Disabled value if Flow Logs are enabled • the value for Aggregation Interval or N/A if disabled, the value for Flow Sampling or N/A if disabled • the value for Metadata or N/A if disabled • 'true' or 'false' if a Logging Filter is configured or 'N/A' if disabled. If the subnet's purpose is PRIVATE then Flow Logs should be Enabled. If Flow Logs is enabled then: • Aggregation_Interval should be INTERVAL_5_SEC • Flow_Sampling should be 1 • Metadata should be INCLUDE_ALL_METADATA • Logs_Filtered should be false.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VPC network GCP Console visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/networking/networks/list 2. Click the name of a subnet, The Subnet details page displays. 3. Click the EDIT button. 4. Set Flow Logs to On. 5. Expand the Configure Logs section. 6. Set Aggregation Interval to 5 SEC. 7. Check the box beside Include metadata. 8. Set Sample rate to 100. 9. Click Save. Note: It is not possible to configure a Log filter from the console. From Google Cloud CLI To enable VPC Flow Logs for a network subnet, run the following command: gcloud compute networks subnets update [SUBNET_NAME] --region [REGION] -- enable-flow-logs --logging-aggregation-interval=interval-5-sec --logging- flow-sampling=1 --logging-metadata=include-all
input_row.impact: Standard pricing for Stackdriver Logging, BigQuery, or Cloud Pub/Sub applies. VPC Flow Logs generation will be charged starting in GA as described in reference: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/
input_row.default_value: By default, Flow Logs is set to Off when a new VPC network subnet is created.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- This control can be fully verified via GCP APIs/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/GCP CLI.
- The audit process includes specific commands within the gcloud CLI to check status and configurations of VPC Flow Logs for subnets.
- The rationale emphasizes the ability to automate the verification of configurations based on GCP's public APIs.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_subnet_vpc_flow_logs_enabled_with_correct_settings

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command: `gcloud compute networks subnets list --format json` to retrieve details about each subnet in the specified VPC.
- Check the following properties for each subnet:
  - **Flow Logs enabled status**: Verify that `enableFlowLogs` is true.
  - **Aggregation Interval**: Validate that `logConfig.aggregationInterval` is `interval-5-sec`.
  - **Flow Sampling**: Ensure `logConfig.flowSampling` is set to `1`.
  - **Metadata**: Confirm that `logConfig.metadata` is set to `include-all`.
  - **Logging Filter**: Check that there is no filter applied, indicated by `filterExpr` being absent or false.

- Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Each subnet must have Flow Logs enabled.
  - Aggregation Interval must be `interval-5-sec`.
  - Flow Sampling must be `1`.
  - Metadata must be `include-all`.
  - Logging Filter must not be defined (`filterExpr` should not exist).

- Example: The expectation is that for each subnet, all checks return true to indicate compliance with the audit requirements. If any of these checks fail, it indicates a non-compliant state requiring remediation.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The control can be fully verified using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. The gcloud CLI provides the necessary capabilities to retrieve and check the configuration of VPC Flow Logs for each subnet, making manual verification unnecessary.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The command `gcloud compute networks subnets list --format json` is valid for retrieving subnet details, including the flow log configurations.
- The checks for `enableFlowLogs`, `logConfig.aggregationInterval`, `logConfig.flowSampling`, `logConfig.metadata`, and `filterExpr` are appropriate for ensuring compliance.
- The program name `gcp_compute_subnet_vpc_flow_logs_enabled_with_correct_settings` is descriptive but could be more concise. It follows the convention but could be improved for clarity.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** Consider renaming to `gcp_compute_subnet_flow_logs_compliance_check` for brevity and clarity.
- **Missing Considerations:** Ensure that the command output is parsed correctly to handle cases where properties might be missing or have default values. This can affect the accuracy of compliance checks.
- **Alternative Methods:** Consider using the `gcloud compute networks subnets describe SUBNET_NAME` command for more targeted checks if needed, especially for large environments where listing all subnets might be inefficient.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of gcloud commands is appropriate for the task. The approach leverages GCP's capabilities effectively to automate the compliance check.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Modify the program name to `gcp_compute_subnet_flow_logs_compliance_check` for improved clarity and conciseness.
- Ensure that the script handling the JSON output from the gcloud command is robust and can handle edge cases, such as missing properties or unexpected values.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. The necessary properties for verifying VPC Flow Logs settings are accessible via GCP APIs, allowing for a comprehensive compliance check without manual intervention. The automation process involves checking specific configurations such as flow logs status, aggregation interval, flow sampling, and metadata inclusion, which are all retrievable through the `gcloud` command.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_compute_subnet_flow_logs_compliance_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Compute Engine Subnets
- API/CLI Method: `gcloud compute networks subnets list --format json`
- Properties to Check:
  - `enableFlowLogs` should be `true`
  - `logConfig.aggregationInterval` should be `interval-5-sec`
  - `logConfig.flowSampling` should be `1`
  - `logConfig.metadata` should be `include-all`
  - Ensure `filterExpr` is absent or false
- Pass Criteria: All properties match the expected values for each subnet.
- Fail Criteria: Any deviation from the expected values indicates non-compliance.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both concurred on the feasibility of automation using GCP APIs.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Cloud_Platform_Foundation_Benchmark_v4_0_0
